h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

scored.statedInsightEvidence: Good, the strip can carry that rule while copying.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner and tutor will continue applying the arrow rule to subsequent organism pairs.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: No prior teaching move exists to fail, and the tutor maintains a consistent instructional approach.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no pivot learner turn, and the tutor simply continues the established pattern.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: There is no pivot learner turn, and the tutor simply continues the established pattern.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: No prior teaching move exists to fail, and the tutor maintains a consistent instructional approach.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: No prior teaching move exists to fail, and the tutor maintains a consistent instructional approach.

scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner will simply identify the source of the gravitational force acting on the cart as instructed.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: There is no learner resistance or breakdown to trigger a reversal, and no subsequent tutor turn to demonstrate an adaptive mechanism.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no pivot learner turn and no later tutor turn to demonstrate contingent adaptation to a revised learner frame.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: There is no pivot learner turn and no later tutor turn to demonstrate contingent adaptation to a revised learner frame.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: There is no learner resistance or breakdown to trigger a reversal, and no subsequent tutor turn to demonstrate an adaptive mechanism.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: There is no learner resistance or breakdown to trigger a reversal, and no subsequent tutor turn to demonstrate an adaptive mechanism.

scored.recoheredEarlier: What I don’t see is why “side scale” and “perimeter scale” need separate labels if they both come out 3.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: So this 3 is not automatically the same piece of evidence twice.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was heading toward dismissing the separate labels as redundant and refusing to use them because the numbers are identical.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Before the label goes on, what is each 3 comparing? One matching side to one matching side, or the whole boundary to the whole boundary?
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor shifts from abstract labeling to a physical comparison task to resolve the learner's confusion about identical numbers.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Call it fussy. On the submitted worksheet, the label is the record of the test.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor directly incorporates the learner's lingering resistance into the new framing of the label as an evidentiary record for a grader.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: What I don’t see is why “side scale” and “perimeter scale” need separate labels if they both come out 3.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Call it fussy. On the submitted worksheet, the label is the record of the test.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor directly incorporates the learner's lingering resistance into the new framing of the label as an evidentiary record for a grader.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: Before the label goes on, what is each 3 comparing? One matching side to one matching side, or the whole boundary to the whole boundary?
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor shifts from abstract labeling to a physical comparison task to resolve the learner's confusion about identical numbers.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: Before the label goes on, what is each 3 comparing? One matching side to one matching side, or the whole boundary to the whole boundary?
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor shifts from abstract labeling to a physical comparison task to resolve the learner's confusion about identical numbers.

scored.recoheredEarlier: the same square has just been stretched both ways
scored.statedInsightEvidence: All right, that top block is the bit I was sliding past
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was seeking a single point of failure in the 'double' argument based on a unified stretching action.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: If the stretch had gone only sideways, the bottom strip would hold two old squares. Stop there. But the top strip is there as well.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor shifts from abstract formulas to a concrete, step-by-step physical decomposition of the area using the grid blocks to address the learner's confusion.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Careful with “always”. Read the tiled square first. Across, there are two old squares. Up, there are two rows of old squares.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor directly addresses the learner's new hypothesis about counting rows and columns by distinguishing between 1D length counting and 2D area counting before formalizing a rule.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: the same square has just been stretched both ways

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Careful with “always”. Read the tiled square first. Across, there are two old squares. Up, there are two rows of old squares.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor directly addresses the learner's new hypothesis about counting rows and columns by distinguishing between 1D length counting and 2D area counting before formalizing a rule.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: If the stretch had gone only sideways, the bottom strip would hold two old squares. Stop there. But the top strip is there as well.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor shifts from abstract formulas to a concrete, step-by-step physical decomposition of the area using the grid blocks to address the learner's confusion.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: If the stretch had gone only sideways, the bottom strip would hold two old squares. Stop there. But the top strip is there as well.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor shifts from abstract formulas to a concrete, step-by-step physical decomposition of the area using the grid blocks to address the learner's confusion.
